<!-- generated by `make bench-docs` (scripts/bench_report.py --page); regenerate on a representative machine, don't hand-edit -->
# Benchmarks

Representative single-machine numbers for **v0.35.0**, committed by hand â€” *not* from CI (shared runners aren't hardware-representative).

Two builds are shown so you can see the from-source upside:

- **portable** â€” `-O3 -march=x86-64-v2 -ffast-math` â€” the shipped PyPI wheel.
- **native** â€” `-O3 -march=native -mprefer-vector-width=256 -ffast-math` â€” `-DDOPPLER_NATIVE=ON`, built from source for this CPU.

**Environment** â€” measured 2026-07-21 15:45:26 UTC, doppler `84c46503`, cc (GCC) 16.1.1 20260625.

- CPU: **AMD Ryzen AI 9 465 w/ Radeon 880M** â€” 20 threads, governor `powersave`, boost on
- OS: 7.1.3-2-cachyos, glibc 2.43
- Libs: Python 3.13.13, NumPy 2.4.5, CMake 4.4.0

Throughput is **MSa/s** (higher is better); latency ops are mean **time/call** (lower is better). *from src* = native vs portable.

> The two builds are measured **interleaved** (alternating runs, per-benchmark best), so *from src* reflects the real build difference. Big gains are vectorizable kernels under AVX-512; overhead-bound benches (tiny per-call work) sit near 0% because the build can't help where Python-call overhead dominates.

## Release history (portable build)

Portable-build throughput across releases â€” the wheel numbers. Comparable only across releases measured on the same machine.
